title: Aldronde
content:
Aldronde 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesThe Deepest, Eastern Sea, Deep Sea
LocationEast of Rhusagos and Patrinor
TypeOcean


Aldronde, the Deep Sea, the Eastern Sea, or Aldronde the Deepest, is a vast sea of Nīmlad to the east of Rhusagos and Patrinor. Little is known about what lies beyond or within it, save for the distant Fire Islands.
Its winds are cold and harsh. They buffet Garnatos regularly, and stir up the waters of the deadly Gurmindār pass.

Etymology
Aldronde is Classical Paternic for “deepest (entity)“. It is the superlative form of the word aldor (“high, deep”), which is being used in this case as a substantive adjective.

title: Great Chasmous Sea
content:
Great Chasmous Sea 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesMiddle Sea, Waters Between, Māprānor, Bazru (P)
LocationBetween Rhusagos and Patrinor
TypeSea
RegionsSamael Sea, Gurmindār, Gates of Neth, the Bight, Bays of Nīmlaugos, Bībūnic Gulf



“It was reported that armies of men had crossed over the Great Chasmous Sea far to the south, bringing with them swords of sharp bronze and many chariots.”

-The Adventures of Wardenhame, “The House of Herald”
The Great Chasmous Sea, sometimes called the Middle Sea or the Waters Between, is the sea separating the continents of Rhusagos and Patrinor.

Description
The Great Chasmous Sea stretches from the Paerāndas in the west to Gurmindār in the east. While it is relatively narrow, it can be difficult to cross in the summer and autumn months, due to the storms that come up from Ponbar in the south. Its waters are generally warm, and the winds that blow from it are fairly wet.
Aldronde and the White Sea are connected by the Great Chasmous Sea. The Green Sea meets with it at the Gates of Neth.
The Samael Sea is a subdivision of the Great Chasmous Sea, defined as a southern arm of the sea separating the island Phagal from Rubea and Makagos.
The Great Chasmous Sea also has several islands: Phagal in its center, the Paerāndas in the west, and Ghinor, which marks the sea’s eastern boundary.

History


                  
                  Blank
                  
                


Dun the Angler lost at sea


Near the end of the Last War, following the Isencurse, the Patrin fled from their ancestral home. That people came to Nīmlaugos and built a great fleet to cross the sea in. The Patrin then came across an island, which they named Phagal, and on it they built the port-town Aegban. From there they journeyed to the continent Patrinor, which they settled in force.
After the founding of the Patrin Empire, a lone fisherman was flung into the Great Chasmous Sea by a southern storm. Some days later he washed onto the shores of Bībūnor. Then, as swiftly as he could, he brought news of this land back to Patrinor. Many colonial ventures from Patrinor crossed the sea at this time, seeking to stake a claim on the realm in the north.

Other Names
The Great Chasmous Sea is often called the Middle Sea and the Waters Between in reference to its location between the White Sea and Aldronde.
The Paternic name for the Great Chasmous Sea is Māprānor (“The Great Chasm”), though the Patrin also have been known to call it Bazru (“Our Sea”).

title: Green Sea
content:
The Green Sea 
                  
                

 Edgewold Forest on the shores of the Green Sea

General Information
LocationSouthwest Rhusagos
TypeInland Sea


The Green Sea is a large inland sea in southern Rhusagos that lies west of Garnatos. It is a hub of trade, and the primary link between the Kingdom of Goblins and Dwarves and Patrinor.
The only waterways into the Green Sea are the narrow straits known as the Gates of Neth. They are named after and watched over by the seaport Neth. The other harbor of note is Kash Ītor, for it marks the beginning of the Dale Road, which passes through Longdale into the Mountain Wall.
Edgewold Forest stands at the Green Sea’s northern shore, and near its southeastern shore there are many hills.

Other Names
The Patrin refer to the Green Sea as Azru Rabesh.

title: Khephnār
content:
Khephnār 
                  
                


General Information
Other NamesWatchwater
LocationFell Mountains
TypeRiver



“The Khephnār would not be easy to cross either. Its fords are few and swift.”

-Jace in The House of Regimen, “Runes Most Foul”
The Khephnār or the Watchwater is a fast-flowing river that runs from the Fell Mountains through western Drakhor into the White Sea. From where it bends towards the west up to the sea, the Watchwater is considered to be the border between Relkor and the Far North. The Watchwater’s brother is Termos in the east, which separates eastern Rhusagos from the Far North.

History
After the First War, the Elder Children raised guard towers and fortifications along its southern banks, fearing Zhē Drūmor’s old realm; but long ago their vigil grew lax, believing the peril to have ended, and the towers were deserted and fell into disrepair.
The party crossed the Watchwater on their journey into the Far North. Shiv Mathor transported all their gear across the river upon a magic platform, but nearly dropped it into the river at the halfway mark.

Etymology
The English translation Watchwater is a calque of the Classical Paternic name Khephnār, which comes from kekheph, kheph (“watch, remain awake”) and -nār (“toponymic suffix for bodies of water”)

title: Loon Sea
content:
The Loon Sea 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesUnlit Sea, Leviathan Sea, Azru Hagrīn (P)
LocationBeyond the Far North
TypePolar sea



“The farthest of all, which are known and spoken of, is Loon; in which there are no days or nights at all. The land there is made up not of proper land nor sea nor air, but a sort of mixture of all three of the consistency of a jellyfish in which one can neither walk nor sail, holding everything together.”

-Kārger in his Geographies
The Loon Sea, the Unlit Sea, or simply Loon, is the most northerly region known to the Elder Children. It is a perilous sea, in places looking more like a white field than water, for it is covered in a lattice of floating ice sheets and glaciers. It is said that only the edge of Tamar  hovers above its horizon, shaped like a sickle, and that its days and nights are blurred together into an endless blue twilight.
A few tribes of the Hords have established small villages on the shores of Loon, where they hunt seals and whales.

Other Names
The sea’s name comes from a constellation known as the “Little Loon”, which contains Nīmlad’s celestial north pole and Siril, the North Star, wherefore it can be used to orient oneself north (toward the sea) at night.
All the Patrin know of the Loon Sea is hearsay and myth. They believe many sea monsters and evil spirits dwell within it, for which reason they sometimes call it Azru Hagrīn (“Leviathan Sea”).

title: Termos
content:
Termos 
                  
                


General Information
Other NamesRiving River
LocationHoarland
TypeRiver


Termos, which means the Riving River, is the principal river of Hoarland, being the border of Khōrgos and Drakhor.
Once it exits the Mountain Wall, its source, the vale of Termos slopes steadily downward and thus has neither falls nor rapids, unlike the Watchwater to the west. It passes through Lake Gharad before hastening toward the Sea of Regimen. The river’s only major crossing is the Termoreth, south of which lies Agos Bahīn, the Corpse-Land.

Etymology
Termos is the agent perfective participle of tezhmi, termi (“separate, divide, split”), translating literally as (“entity that divided something”).

title: Tungril
content:
Tungril 
                  
                

 One of the tributaries of Tungril

General Information
Other NamesSouthflood
LocationFar South
TypeRiver


Tungril is broadest and fullest of all rivers in the Wide World, and it is thought it might be longer than even Lash. It marks the western boundary between Patrinor and the Far South up to its sources in the Far Mountains, making a total run of about 1300–1400 miles.
Ion Ephel, a colony of the Patrin Empire, was established by the river’s mouth, which feeds into the White Sea at the northern edge of Ponbar.

Etymology
Tungril is Classical Paternic for “Southflood”, from et (“south”) and negril (“great river, flood”).

title: White Sea
content:
The White Sea 
                  
                

 A Trogmunder catamaran

General Information
Other NamesFrog Sea, Western Sea, Azru Sok, Azru Rabīn (P)
LocationWest of Rhusagos and Patrinor
TypeOcean
RegionsSargāzat, Alensic Channel, Marble Gulf, Gulf of Drāmūz, Azrāken, Ponbar, Home Islands


The White Sea, also called the Frog Sea and the Western Sea, is the ocean to the west of Rhusagos and Patrinor. Beyond it lie the Moonless Lands of the Far West.
Of all the Children, only the Trogmunders have explored the whole of the White Sea, for the Home Islands, from whence they hail, lie near its center.
Hurricanes develop in the warmer waters of the White Sea’s south near Ponbar. In late summer and through autumn, these storms travel north toward Patrinor, and sometimes into the Great Chasmous Sea, making maritime travel very hazardous.

title: Meldutha
content:
Meldutha-hoth-Mīth 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesMeldutha, Fortress Beneath the Void
LocationRankaros
TypeFortress
DescriptionVast and very cold, with pits extending deep into the earth

People and History
InhabitantsZhē Drūmor, the Witen, and his other slaves and servants
EventsGreat Battle


Meldutha-hoth-Mīth (more commonly Meldutha), or the Fortress Beneath the Void, was a mansion of Zhē Drūmor in Rankaros, his realm in the Far North.
It was the greatest and most calamitous by far of all his barracks, bastions, dungeons, palaces, towers, and pits, whose gates were iron, whose breath was thunder and rime. Blizzards belched out of its lofty chimneys, and filth pooled in its nethermost halls.
Zhē Drūmor used Meldutha as his base of operations for the whole of the First War until the Great Battle, when the Kembar assailed his fortress. At that time, they broke its doors and unroofed it; Zhē Drūmor hid in the uttermost pit, but they chained him and flung him into Dor Lazban, which he has never escaped.
After the battle, the Kembar abandoned Meldutha, leaving it to be forgotten.

Etymology
Meldutha-hoth-Mīth is a Classical Paternic name. It means “Fortress Beneath the Void”, being a combination of melduth (“That Which Is Not, Void”) in the locative case, the postposition hoth (“under”), and mīth (“fortified town, fort, fortress, citadel”).

title: Seat
content:
The Seat 
                  
                



General Information
LocationSummarch, on High Hill
TypeFortress

People and History
InhabitantsWestermen
EventsSiege of the SeatReturn of the Kembar


The Seat is the great keep of the Kings of Westerland. It stands on the peak of High Hill, the mound about which much of Summarch, the capital city of Westerland, was built.

Description
The Seat rests atop High Hill, a promontory with sheer cliffs on two of its sides. A curtain wall rings High Hill’s peak as a shield for the keep, though parts of it are in disrepair and have been patched with wood. A ditch has been dug in front of the wall also, with its only crossing point being a stone bridge that connects to the wall’s sole gate.
Within that wall there are multiple thatch buildings and other constructions of practical purpose, including a stable, larders and storerooms, a smithy, and a sparring field.
The keep itself is comprised of two buildings:

The Main Hall, which houses the living arrangements of the King, his family, and his servants; his throne room, where he holds court; and many other smaller rooms and cellars.
The Point, a defensive tower attached to the Main Hall by a high-up bridge; it has been converted into a prison for mages.


History
After Daemar I removed his court to Summarch, he began the Seat’s construction, though it wasn’t completed till the middle years of the reign of Daeka, his daughter, c. 850 A.B.
The Seat was the home of Selwin the Last in the 14th century A.B.
In the summer of 1374 A.B., two Allhammers, Frösted Flækes and Guantanamo Michael, were dispatched to the Seat in order to question Lord Selwin regarding his potential involvement in the murder of Grandmaster Hagar. The pair entered the Main Hall, wherein Frösted took possession of Keenest, a prized heirloom of the House of Regimen. Selwin then came upon them and, unaware of their identities and interpreting the act as theft, ordered their arrest. During the confrontation Guantanamo hurled a fireball into the ceiling in an attempt to intimidate the guards, but the display failed to scatter them. Taking this as further provocation, Selwin had the Allhammers imprisoned in the Point. Though Selwin afterward claimed the Allhammers had assaulted him and his retainers, the arrests further inflamed public unrest already festering within Summarch.
Within the Point, Frösted and Guantanamo were separated from each other. The Hord was made to share a cell with another prisoner named Renaud while Guantanamo (given his leechcraft) was isolated in the highest cell and drained of most of his blood. Eventually, a bird alighted on the floor beside Guantanamo. Swiftly and desperately he seized it, crushing its neck and tearing it open for the blood within. Using the gore for fuel, Guantanamo was able to open his cell door, find Frösted, and escape. As a consequence, Renaud was also sprung free, although he disappeared shortly after.
Later that same year, in the autumn, a rebellion led by Rennick, the new Grandmaster of the Allhammers, broke out in the streets of Summarch against Lord Selwin, who was forced behind the walls of the Seat. The keep was then put to siege.
Through a combination of alchemical explosives and traditional siege engines, the Seat’s main gate was knocked down, the bridge was seized, and a final battle was fought. After the battle, which ended in Rennick’s victory, Selwin was executed in his Main Hall and his titles were usurped by the Allhammer, who then claimed the Seat for his own.

title: Winehall
content:
The Winehall, also known as Pherūkhar (P), is the Allhammers’ base of operations. It is located in Summarch beside the banks of the Stony River.
The building is a longhouse containing two floors: one at the ground level and one below. The first floor is a single, large room, containing hearths, roasting spits, and tables for feasting. All public counsels and elections are held within it.
The basement floor has sleeping quarters for any resting or visiting Allhammers, a kitchen with chimneys leading above ground, an armory, and an extensive wine cellar.

title: Dor Lazban
content:
Dor Lazban, also known as the Abyss, is the prison Zhē Drūmor was cast into after his defeat in the First War. It was made by Hesphek somewhere deep beneath the earth, in a location known only to him.
The Paternic name Dor Lazban translates literally as “Black Pit”, from dor (“black”) and lazban (“mine, quarry, pit”).

title: Gurmakhar
content:
Gurmakhar 
                  
                


General Information
Other NamesThe Hall of Passing, Īzlagurmakīmkhar, The Hall of Good and Bad Deaths, Doremil
LocationOutside the Wide World

People and History
InhabitantsDoremil, the souls of recently-dead Children


Gurmakhar (“Hall of Passing”) is the dwelling of Doremil, the God of death. It is a land apart from the Wide World, built to house the spirits of the dead, for concerning them Doremil is held to be deepest in understanding. But Gurmakhar is only a resting place, for the spirits of the Children are transient and must depart from the universe eventually, to which they are but visitors; and whither they go or why even Doremil has not discovered.
It is said that Gimīlhizar sealed and set guards on all the ingresses into Gurmakhar from Rhusagos and Patrinor. There are rumors, however, of untouched entrances on the other side of the world, in the distant Moonless Lands of the Far West.

Other Names
Gurmakhar is translated as “Hall of Passing”. It is technically a shortening of the land’s full name Izlagurmakīmkhar, which means “Hall of Good and Bad Deaths”.
Some refer to Gurmakhar as Doremil after its creator and keeper.

title: Nīmlad
content:
Nīmlad 
                  
                



General Information
PronunciationP: [ˈniːm.läd] (NEEM-lahd)
Other NamesWide World, Great Work, Kingdom of Earth
LocationMeleth
TypeWorld
DescriptionHabitable moon orbiting a gas giant
RegionsTethros (Rhusagos, Patrinor, Home Islands), Far North, Far South, Moonless Lands

People and History
InhabitantsKembar, Elder Children, Younger Children



“Hence they came to a globed world, which is now named Nīmlad, the Sky and Sea; and they entered into it, and their power was poured into the World, and became bound to it, and flowered and withered it.”

-Rogondēndāl
Nīmlad, also known as the Wide World and the Earth, is the world as a whole. It was originally a barren moon within Meleth until it was discovered and shaped by the Kembar.
Nīmlad is the home of Men, Elves, Dwarves, Trogmunders, and other races.

Description
Nīmlad is a moon orbiting about the purple-blue gas giant called Tamar, to which it is tidally locked. Thusly, from any location on Nīmlad’s surface, Tamar appears forever fixed at one point in the sky. A day on Nīmlad is equivalent in time to the moon’s orbital period around Tamar.
From the correct latitudes (such as those between the bounds of Rhusagos and Patrinor), Tamar eclipses the sun for about an hour sometime in the afternoon. This period is known as the dark hour. Also, time can be told based on how much of Tamar is in shadow at any particular moment.
The Children define their year as the time elapsed between Yuledays (a 365-day period). Each Yuleday, extreme aurora displays light up every sky on Nīmlad. This phenomenon is caused by the moon slipping into the boundary of Tamar’s magnetosphere at the conclusion of its apsidal period (which takes a year to complete), leading to Nīmlad’s atmosphere experiencing more solar wind than usual for about a day every year.
At this point in time, the Wide World’s degree of technological advancement is roughly equivalent to that of our late Iron Age. Chariots have only recently been phased out of militaries in favor of cavalry, and dromons and catamarans are the largest watercraft on the sea. However, bronze on Nīmlad is still far more commonly used than iron. This is due to the fact that tin and copper (the combination of which creates bronze alloys) are far more abundant on this world’s surface than they are on Earth, while iron is relatively rare. Steel, therefore, is an extremely valuable metal, and few know how to make it.
Etymology
Nīmlad means “Sky and Sea”, being derived from Classical Paternic nīmil (“sky”). It is an artifact of when the dual number existed in the languages. More specifically, it comes from a specific usage of the dual known as the elliptical dual, wherein two entities associated with each other could be referred to using the dual form of one of that pair. In this instance, the pairing was the sky and the sea.

title: Siril
content:
Blank
                  
                


Siril written in the Gindas. Note that the first glyph, “moon”, conveys no phonetic meaning, and is used here only to indicate that the second glyph, “pivot, axis”, refers to something cosmological.


Colored red, Siril is the brightest star in the night sky. Its position is fixed above true north within the constellation Little Loon, for which reason it’s commonly called the North Star. Siril is used as a navigational aid by all sailors, travelers, explorers, and wanderers.

Etymology
Siril translates as “pivot, fulcrum, axis” from the verb ser, sir (“spin, turn, rotate”), the idea being that the night sky seemingly rotates around it.

title: Tamar
content:
“Night had deepened. A cool wind blew from the west, and far away the dark clouds had finally dried up and broken over the downs. Their tattered remains snaked away eastward, revealing the moon at last. It was almost half waxed, filling the sky with a vivid purple-blue glow.”

-The House of Regimen, “The Flight from the Inn”
Tamar, the Moon (as the Children refer to it), is a celestial object ever-present in the skies of Nīmlad. It is a gas giant of purplish-blue hue. Nīmlad, which is tidally locked to Tamar, is Tamar’s only satellite.

title: Tethros
content:
Tethros is the inhabited, civilized world, as opposed to the barbaric wild. The term Tethros is different from Nīmlad, which refers to the world as a physical whole.
The word itself is the imperfective participle of tethus, thus (“to dwell”), so in a literal sense it means “entity being dwelt in”.

title: That Which Is
content:
That Which Is, also called Creation, the Heavens, the Universe, Meleth, and Meldad is all of existence: all forms of matter and energy, from motes of dust to stars and galaxies.
Here is the beginning of the universe as described in the Rogondēndāl:

“But then from out of the Void, a new thing was birthed: That Which Is; and it was named Creation, the first to have ever been. Its offspring were the dust and the stars, and they poured out into the Void, and it was made not void. Light shone from the stars, which were as lamps in the darkness, and from the dust came the matters to build the lands and fields of Creation’s kingdom. Thusly was Meleth, the realm of all existence, wrought.

The Kembar were birthed far away amidst the stars of Creation. For eons they explored it, until they decided to create and settle a world of their own: Nīmlad. The shape, duration, extent, and contents of That Which Is are not known to the Children, whose lore of it contains only that which the Kembar have told them. Magic is essentially the ability to command and change the physical properties of That Which Is to a very limited degree.
Outside the bounds of Creation lies its opposite: That Which Is Not, more commonly referred to as the Void.

Etymology
The Paternic term Meleth technically refers only to outer space, but speakers use it as a metonym for the whole universe extremely frequently.
The proper term for the universe as a whole is Meldad, an elliptical noun that means “Heaven and Earth”.

title: That Which Is Not
content:
Blank
                  
                


Gimīlhizar finding the Void



“They see the darkness behind the stars and believe that it is all the outer world is. They hate its peril.”

-Doremil in the Rogondēndāl
In the beginning, there was That Which Is Not, which in the Wide World is called the Long Nothing, and the Void, for in it were all things that were not, that are not, and that will never come to be.
Cosmologically, it is all that exists beyond the confines of That Which Is, its antithesis. The Void is nothingness distilled; it is not even empty, as emptiness implies the existence of a space that can be filled.
The study of the applications of That Which Is Not is known as magicks or dwimmercraft. Prophecy, the calling up of spirits, ritual magic, unnatural longevity, and the corruption of life and earth are powers that can be granted by engagement with the Void, although those who do so are inevitably tainted by seeds of madness.
Gimīlhizar, who became the Mad One, was the first to encounter and the first to master the Void. In preparation for the First War, he conjured spirits out of it and released them into the Wide World, most notably the Líches and the Gands.

Other Names
Melduth is the Classical Paternic name for That Which Is Not. It is derived from the elements meleth (“outer space, That Which Is”) and uth (“outside of”).
That Which Is Not is also known as:

Void
Long Nothing
Outer World
Darkness Behind the Stars

title: Far Mountains
content:
Far Mountains 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesMārosh Ephel (P)
LocationSpanning the length of the Far South
TypeMountain range

People and History
InhabitantsUnknown, if any


Mārosh Ephel, which translates as the Far Mountains, is the longest mountain range known to the Children. It runs east-west and forms the chief part of the conventional boundary between Patrinor and the Far South. The river Tungril, which marks the western half of the Far South’s border, has all its sources in the Far Mountains.
Cloud forests cover many of its slopes, and it has a few volcanoes, most notably about the Horned Pass.

Etymology
Mārosh Ephel is comprised of the noun mārosh (“mountain range”) and the adjective ephil (“far, distant, remote”).

title: Fell Mountains
content:
Fell Mountains 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesMārosh Mālder (P)
LocationFar North, surrounding the south of Rankaros
TypeMountain range

People and History
InhabitantsWiten, Younger Children


In the north of the world, Zhē Drūmor in ages past reared Mārosh Mālder, the Fell Mountains, as a fence to his citadel of Meldutha; and they stand to this day upon the borders of the regions of everlasting frost in a great curve from east to west.
The Khephnār, one of the boundary rivers of the Far North has its source in the Fell Mountains. They also feed the Sea of Regimen and briny Ūgaden.
Lugal the Dragon vanished into the Fell Mountains at the end of the Last War, fearing wrath and retribution.

Etymology
Mārosh Mālder is made up of mārosh (“mountain range”) and the inanimate form of māldor (“evil, wicked”).

title: Mountain Wall
content:
The Mountain Wall 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesMārosh Aegus (P), Wall Mountains
LocationEastern Rhusagos and Drakhor
TypeMountain range

People and History
InhabitantsDwarves, Goblins, Hords, Younger Children



“In the end, Herald’s people alone followed him into the north. There, they found a high mountain range that rose up and around them like a fence, which they called the Mountain Wall.”

-The Adventures of Wardenhame, “The House of Herald”
The Mountain Wall (Mārosh Aegus in Classical Paternic) is an immense mountain range that runs along the eastern shores of Rhusagos and the Far North.

Geography
The mountain range is about 900 kilometers (186 leagues) in length, meeting with the Snow Gulf in the north and approaching the Green Sea to its south. At its southern end, the Mountain Wall splits into two lesser ranges: the Spur and the Hollow Hills. Longdale (35 leagues long) is the valley between these two chains.
Surrounding Lands
The Darkholt and Edgewold Forest are the two great forests nestled against the Mountain Wall. Both are known to be deadly.
On its western side lies a region of foothills and gorges known as the Underwall. Some of the Lake-Elves settled there, fleeing the War for the Lake, but their villages were abandoned and fell into ruin sometime around 900 A.B.
Hoarland borders the Mountain Wall’s northwest slopes where it bleeds into the Far North.
Rivers
Many rivers have their sources issuing from the Mountain Wall. From north to south, the largest of these are:

Termos
Fenny
Swanwater
Galder
Gruma


Inhabitants
The Kingdom of Goblins and Dwarves rules over much of the Mountain Wall, its dominion extending even into the Underwall and Longdale. The Goblins and Dwarves, being friends, have delved for themselves great palaces and mansions in the mountains. Vastest of all that kingdom’s works is Clickerhall-by-Goblin-Hole, where the Chief under the Wall resides. South of it is the city Ernbury, which was built within a vale instead of carved into the rock. It watches over Longdale and the Dale Road.
Some tribes of the Hords, a Mannish people, dwell in the northern foothills of the Mountain Wall.
It is said that Vampires roost in the recesses of the Hollow Hills.

Etymology
The Paternic name for the Mountain Wall, Mārosh Aegus, consists of mārosh (“mountain range”) and aegus (“city wall, palisade”).

title: Far North
content:
Far North 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesDrakhor (P)
LocationNīmlad, north of Rhusagos
TypeContinent
RegionsRankaros, Fell Mountains, Hoarland, Snow Gulf, Cape of Drakhor, Mountain Wall

People and History
InhabitantsHords, Witen, Swams, Younger Children
EventsFirst War  Great Battle  Last War  Wardenhame’s Adventures



“It is the lurking-land of many ancient and evil things; Werewolves make haunts of its woods, hoarwyrms tunnel beneath its snows, Witch-Birds hold covens in its hills.”

-Tamūlae Sūl, “Of Rhusagos and Its Realms”
The Far North, called Drakhor by those in the south, is a desolate, cold region beyond Rhusagos. There the sunlight is always wan. It was the land of Zhē Drūmor during the First War.

Description
The Far North is separated from Rhusagos by the Sea of Regimen and two wide rivers: Khephnār in the west and Termos in the east, which are translated the Watchwater and the Riving River respectively.
Its northwestern shores are dominated by the curve of the Fell Mountains, beyond which lies the evil island Rankaros. The southern slopes of the mountains are densely forested.
The eastern half of the Far North is much more bare and flat. Most of it is known as Hoarland, which has the twin lakes Gharad and Ūgaden in its center. The Mountain Wall is on Hoarland’s eastern flank, running past it to the Snow Gulf, a large body of water dividing the Cape of Drakhor from the rest of the land.
A multitude of icy firths and fjords run all along the northeast coast, even cutting into the Fell Mountains in places.
Beyond even the Far North, there lies the little-understood Loon Sea, a seemingly endless field of floating ice sheets and glaciers.

History
After his Fall, Zhē Drūmor poured his magicks into the Far North, effecting a curse of bitter, wasting cold. It was here that he established his kingdom, and where all the foul creatures of his design lived and bred.
Following the end of the First War, many monsters, Kindreds of the Younger Children, and Witen made their homes in the wilds of the Far North.
Once, the Elder Children patrolled the Far North’s edges, fearing what lay beyond; but their vigil has grown lax of late, believing the peril to have ended, and the guard towers are abandoned and in disrepair. Alas, the corruptions of Zhē Drūmor are woven into the earth forever, and Drakhor slumbers but is not defeated.

Map


Etymology
Drakhor, the Classical Paternic name for the Far North, comes primarily from the word drakh (“north, north wind”).

title: Hoarland
content:
Hoarland 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesGhazdaukos (P)
LocationFar North, about Lakes Gharad and Ūgaden
TypeRegion
DescriptionCold moors and uplands

People and History
InhabitantsHords


Hoarland is mostly sparse moors and uplands, although there are snowy forests about Termos and the dells of Mārosh Aegus, which stretches into the Far North. In that country’s center are the twin lakes Ūgaden and Gharad. The River Termos spills out of Gharad, hastening towards the Sea of Regimen; but from Ūgaden no rivers flow, and only fish can drink its brine, as the lake is polluted with salt.
The Mannish peoples known as the Hords call Hoarland their home. Their largest villages are Sjefen, Kolin, and Hvalstrond.

Etymology
Hoar is an adjective meaning “of a grayish-white color”. It is found also in the word hoarfrost.
The Paternic translation Ghazdaukos means “Snowy Land” from ghazdauk (“snowy, gray-haired”).

title: Rankaros
content:
Rankaros 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesHell, Realm of Monsters, Island of Dark Ice
LocationFarthest land of the Far North
TypeIsland
CapitalMeldutha

People and History
InhabitantsWiten, Men, slaves, and many monsters
GovernanceZhē Drūmor


Behind the walls of Mārosh Mālder lies the most foul and feared land of every age: Rankaros, Realm of Monsters, Island of Dark Ice, Hell. Within its confines, there are no lamps or fires, for Zhē Drūmor hated all things that make light, and he banished them from his holdings, whither they never returned.

History
From his icy land of Rankaros, Gimīlhizar, chief of the Kembar, governed his kingdom. There he could perceive much that went on within Nīmlad. The sky was his watchman, and he had as servants many spies to bear him news.
But then through envy and fear, he fell into shadow. Preparing for war, he created Mārosh Mālder as a defensive wall for his realm. Then he erected his mansions, and to create his armories, he dug exceedingly deep, so that his subterranean furnaces could use the heat of the Earth rather than employ any open flame. The first and greatest of his fortresses was Meldutha, home to hosts of demons, Witen, and other creatures.
The First War ended with the Great Battle, in which the host of the Kembar assailed Rankaros and overthrew Zhē Drūmor, who was forever imprisoned.
Etymology
Rankaros means “Frigid Land”. Its chief element is the adjective rankar (“ice-cold, freezing, frigid”).

title: Far South
content:
Far South 
                  
                



General Information
Other NamesGhahuros (P)
LocationNīmlad, south of Patrinor
TypeContinent
RegionsHome Islands, Ponbar, Far Mountains, Horned Pass, Gar Sea

People and History
InhabitantsTrogmunders
EventsSundering of the Trogs


Beyond the river Tungril and the Far Mountains is Ghahuros, the Far South, which the Patrin have never explored. Its forests are dense and hot, and the air is so thick with moisture that rain falls out of it most of the year. On the left bank of Tungril is the coast called Ponbar, whence come the great storms. Strange beasts of tooth, beak, and ivory rove there, but whether they are feathered lizards, scaled birds, or something in between, not even the Trogmunders can say.
The Home Islands, the lands of those Trogs who followed Lalo, lie in the Far South, surrounded on all sides by the White Sea.

Etymology
The name Ghahuros means “Savage Land”, being a derivation of ghahur (“foreigner, barbarian, savage”).
